speaker: interviewee
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] .

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Piotr Kadlcik. K-A-D-L-C-I-K. I'm conducting an interview with Mr. Freddy Diament, Auschwitz, April 19, 1996. So please tell-- spell your name and-- please.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Freddy Diament. D-I-A-M-E-N-T. I came from concentration camp Sachsenhausen with a group of-- of 1,200 prisoners. We arrived here-- I will never forget it in my life-- on October the 22nd, 1942. It was a place just like this. We were unloaded at the ramp. We came in cattle cars with a lot of hullaboo, with dogs and screaming of SS people. And, of course, fellow prisoners that pushed us out of the cars. On the way to Auschwitz, especially in the last few hours, there was such anxiety. We really were not sure will we live or will we die. We were so-- I remember how I was afraid. I was scared to death. I was clinging to my father and my older brother. They were with me together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And to the last minute, we didn't know what is going to happen to us because although we had no exact information, but in the concentration of Sachsenhausen, we knew one thing. That terrible things are going to happen in Auschwitz. So the question was, will it be death? Will it be life?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took us first run, run, run, run, you know. The typical push in Auschwitz. And we ran to the shower rooms. There, we were all-- we got a bit of soap. We showered. They cut our hair again so that we were really totally shaved. And then what we didn't know-- we were Polish prisoners. And he said, give your arm, in very rough tones. And before we knew it, they took like a pen with a little needle at the bottom, and they were sticking at us. And it was hurting. And I was young then. I-- I pulled back my--

speaker: interviewer
text: How old you were at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 18 already. But you know, there's something amazing. The years in Auschwitz, there was no maturity. You stayed the way-- I-- I was 15 when I arrived. And I felt like 15, in all honesty, because nothing happens besides-- in a different universe. Auschwitz was-- Sachsenhausen was a universe as if you're on a different planet.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you pulled away your hand, in a very cynical way they say to us, you consider this suffering? You will find out what Auschwitz is. You will suffer plenty.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, after we got our-- our tattoo, but still it was very degrading. That much-- not intellectually. Emotionally, we felt, my god. From a human being, I turn into a number. That much, we could understand. And then to spite us, they demanded that the whole transport, 1,200 people, had leave the wash room and were to stay-- stand on the outside all night naked. I remember at that time already, at least 20 to 30 people died because it was a cold winter night. It was October.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any-- apart from your family-- any names of the people that were with you at the time? Any relatives or just friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, my father was with us. He only was 50 years old. And because when you stay in a concentration camp three years, you have connection. We really-- my brother and I, we were very proud then, succeeded in-- in protecting our father. And then my brother was a year older. His name was Leo Diament. He was a year older than me. And the next morning after dawn, the infamous Dr. Mengele came. And-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] --we had to-- how do you say-- pass by him just when you go in a slaughter house like you look at cattle. Another case there where we felt the degradation that-- you know. Also Sachsenhausen was a terrible camp. But they did not develop-- they were not sophisticated enough to develop those-- those method where you can give a person a feeling that is not human being anymore. Then he looked at us. And he didn't even say, go here. Literally with his thumb, that means you go to the other side, and that means you are worthy to work. And what we didn't know is those that were worthy to work were put in Barrack 8. I remember-- that will I never forget. I was in Block 8. And I think we stayed there for two days. And then suddenly, the order came. In German, they call this [GERMAN]. To present yourself and be marched to a place that was just a camp, and it was Auschwitz III. At that time, they called it-- they didn't call it Buna. They called it Monowitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we marched like this for five weeks. And we were worked-- in Monowitz, we worked. Some of us were right away selected. They ask, who is a carpenter? Who is a-- a-- a mechanic? Who is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we knew. That we have learned in three year concentration camp. If you are-- you have a metier, a profession, that is already a half salvation. And everybody became suddenly a carpenter, builder, a mason, what have you, just to work-- work where you didn't-- there was chicanery. But the majority, if there was not enough work, there were-- there were the infamous Polish over-kapo Bolek. I will never forget his name. He was literally a murderer, that man.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the last name of his?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Bolek.

speaker: interviewer
text: Bolek.

speaker: interviewee
text: Every-- the whole camp in horror knew his name. And what we did sometimes is we took our-- I call it coat. You know, the striped coat. And we shoveled soil on that. And then we had to run in a circle, and the kapos were beating us, and the SS was beating us, and throwing it there. And the next day, they throw it from there to there. It was just chicanery. It wasn't really constructive work. But others built barracks. This civilians, they helped.

speaker: interviewer
text: I would like to go back to the selection, what we're talking-- you were talking about Dr. Mengele and the selection.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You told that he was selecting people but-- by putting--

speaker: interviewee
text: By putting.

speaker: interviewer
text: --up or down. Did you already know what does it mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. That, we didn't know. Later on. You think, what did he do? And we had our suspicion because the sign is very symbolic. That means you're going [YIDDISH], so to speak, of course, Yiddish. And that means that we had a chance at least for time being to survive.

speaker: interviewee
text: Unfortunately, my father at that time was separated from us. And-- and he stayed in Auschwitz little longer. And I-- I was told by a man in Israel that was together my father that within a few weeks-- can you hear me? That within a few weeks, he was literally strained to death.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it was at that time-- I'm talking about in 1942-- that kapos literally were doing nothing but beating up half-- prisoners. That's what-- that what they were for there. Anyway, after five weeks, they told us, now you are being moved. You go back to over there. They were moved to Monowitz, which later became known as the Buna camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Freddy, I think that now we'll finish for this part and we'll come to the Auschwitz--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Buna.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to the entrance. Well, first to the entrance of the-- of the camp--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and then we'll go to Buna.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So please tell us where we are now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right now, we are close to the entrance to Auschwitz. And this same sign is in every concentration camp. A similar reception I had three years earlier, when I was 15 years old also in October of 1939 when we came to Sachsenhausen. And here again. We have 'Arbeit Macht Frei.' What I didn't know is that besides getting the shower, disinfection, and a few clap and a few push, what have you, that suddenly I went through and somebody literally grabbed my arm. Out of nowhere. I didn't even see those people. But they spoke to us in Polish, so I knew they were Polish prisoners. They didn't talk any German, any other language. And before I knew it, he took like a pen. It looked like a pen. And while he kept my hand, I saw a-- a needle came out of that pen. He pushed me with a finger. And before I knew it, I had a number on my arm. And it hurt. And I was pulling away my arm. And in a very nasty and cynical way, this Polish prisoner told me, wait a minute. Don't be so sensitive. This is peanuts, so to speak. There's nothing. You would find out what suffering is. This is Auschwitz, and don't you ever forget it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is-- what do think, how much time it took to make-- to make such number?

speaker: interviewee
text: Seconds. It-- it went so fast, I didn't know what was happening to me. What-- what I did understand almost emotionally that suddenly you are number. Although we had numbers on our forefront in Sachsenhausen too, in this concentration-- but on the arm.

speaker: interviewer
text: It-- it gave me a feeling like a piece of cattle. Because cattle was marked all over the world. And now I'm a number. So in other words, you had a feeling, will you ever get out of this here? That dark feeling, we all got. So after we got our number, they forced us to stand and to-- to pass by. And they had the nerve-- we were 1,200 people in this transport-- to let us stay all night naked outside in four rows. I remember it like yesterday.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was so cold. It was October 1944. It was a cold winter. And nobody was allowed even to go to the bathroom. And excuse the expression, they had to do it on-- on the spot. And the next morning, they pulled us together. And who came, the infamous Dr. Mengele.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Dr. Mengele, when he passed by, looked at us just like-- like people look in a slaughterhouse on cattle. Is he fat enough. Does it make sense? Should he be still fat, this piece of cattle? And rather than talking, he took his thumb-- I will never forget it in my life-- and made one sign.

speaker: interviewee
text: This means to the left, and this means to the right. At that time, we didn't know exactly what's going to happen then. But we knew those where the thumb showed down, nothing good will happen to them. And among them was also my father. And my brother and myself were sent to the side. And I had a terrible feeling at that time because we literally succeeded with all the effort that we could to protect our father for three years in a concentration camp. It was not easy. Many times, we had to-- to-- literally to put him out on a work detail that we knew that a day's work can mean his death. Once we pull him out and send him to the-- to the sick barrack to get an injection. We also had enough connection to pull him out. In here, we were separated. We knew right away that's probably the last time we ever saw our father, and how right we were. Because a few weeks later, we found out that he was literally beaten to death in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can--

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had a terrible guilt feeling at that time. That somehow we saved ourselves and didn't take care of our father. And that-- that guilt feeling lasts with me years in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you please show us the number that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Yeah. Unfortunately, I-- I-- I'm a-- I do my own gardening. I scratch myself. My number is 69927. I have the feeling that no more than maybe seven people are alive with a number like this. This is a very low number. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: So please tell us where we are now and what--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: --was the life place like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: As I said, after the selection, those where Mengele showed to the right, we were dressed in pajamas, the typical garb that any prisoner in Auschwitz had. And we were marched to Block 8. I remember very well. Outside was standing-- they called this in the camp [GERMAN]. [GERMAN] means [GERMAN], or professional criminal. What the Nazis did, they pulled out all the Germans that were convicted for capital punishment like rape, murder, and they became the internal administration in the camps. And believe me, they were qualified. They were murderers. And here, they had the chance, whatever they wanted to do, they could do it without any punishment, without any responsibility. They hadn't to report to anybody. On contrary, they were rewarded. The more they beat to death, the more they tortured us, the more they stood up in terms of their prestige. I remember they were very rough with us. And at that time, there were no beds. There were no bunks. Apparently, this was a kind of transition block. We were sleeping on the floor like sardines. Head to feet, head to feet. And sometimes it was so tight that literally at night, they give a command, now you can turn to the right, because you just cannot sleep all the time on the one side.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was the life at that time? Not very much different from the life in Sachsenhausen. The only difference was in Sachsenhausen, we were veterans. After three years, if you had the ability to survive three years in any concentration camp, you were considered something like a celebrity, practically.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here, we had to start all over again. And that was frightening. Because I wondered if I will be able to duplicate what I'd accomplished in-- in Sachsenhausen. Here, I was just a newcomer, and newcomers were treated very roughly.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, even for the roll call. Every morning we had to stand up here in the cold, sometimes for hours. And we had to take also our dead and half-dead people. We had to schlep them on the-- on the-- how do you say [GERMAN] in-- in English? I don't remember. On blanket.

speaker: interviewee
text: On a blanket. Because it has to-- exactly the figure had to come out. If God forbid one was missing, it was the worst that could happen to you. We were standing sometimes for 12, for 15, and even for 18 hours without food, without being allowed to go to the bathroom.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we as prisoners, we-- if I-- of-- of-- how so I say this. We-- we were glad that everything was exactly to the point that everybody was here. And sometimes if the SS [GERMAN], they call them. The people that were in charge of each barrack. They call them [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewee
text: They were either sergeants, non-commissioned officers. If they were in a good mood, again they called this sport. Although there is not much room, enough was we had to roll from one side to the other, and then from the other side to this side again. And every time there came two, three, sometimes four [GERMAN], and they were trampling on us. Here, I don't remember that they ever came with rifles, as I-- I said, they came in Sachsenhausen. But all those sports resulted in people to be hurt that badly, that it was sure that eventually they will wind up in the crematoria. The food was very, very minimal. Black coffee, a small piece of bread, and a dishwater soup. That all what we got. Now, thank God after the first few days-- by the way, when you sit here all day long, an hour seems like a day. A day seems like a month. Time is moving very slowly, and you start brooding. I mean, you start brooding. You only think about the worst. And it was very demoralizing. And we were happy that after two-- three or four days, we were called one morning, and we were marched like all the other work details that always march every day to the-- to the gate. And we were marching, I would say, sometimes it took us an hour, to a new camp that was just being built. And that camp was Monowitz or Auschwitz III.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can we come back to-- to-- to time when you were here, on a kind of a quarantine. Can-- do you remember any stories from that time? That all the-- all the people were getting demoralized by being here and doing nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Auschwitz had such a bad name that although we didn't know exactly-- as I-- as I said before. But in the other concentration camps in-- in Germany, we knew that very bad things happen in Auschwitz. That we knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we never were sure, are we destined to live and to work, or are we destined to go to the gas chambers? When we were here, within hours we knew that in Auschwitz people are being gassed. That secret was blown out of it right away.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the Polish prisoners that received us let us know, don't be so sure of yourself. Maybe at the end, nobody will survive. So we knew. And that-- that had-- that-- that put a fear in you, that-- you know, you really don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Will I be alive in the next hour? Will I be alive the next day? And it's terribly demoralizing. Thank God I had my brother with me, which helped. We-- Me give us courage to each other. And then we-- a few weeks later, we-- we-- we did not get an exact message. But we knew there is no way that our father was already 50 years old and can survive such chicanery. So for us, it was clear. And-- and silently we were saying courage. That I remember too. After my father. Because we knew there is not-- not a chance in a million that he is still alive. But that was Auschwitz for us. And you were at-- at that time were still here at this block.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. We-- all the time until we were transferred to Buna. After five weeks there were enough barracks ready that people were transferred already to stay in Buna, and we were assigned to work in the new IG farm plant. In my opinion, eventually it became the largest industrial complex in all of Europe. That was the place where they tried to produce gasoline from coal. And Buna-- Buna was essentially a replacement for Rab, because without wheels you cannot start a war and you cannot continue a war. So that what we did in Buna.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I will talk about it later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. How many people were in block like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would assume we had to be about 800 people. 400 on each side. There are two wings. And as I said, there were no bunks. We slept on the floor with a thin blanket on top of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what kind of a bathroom facilities that were here?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was inside a bathroom facility, very primitive. But you-- you take what you get.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about place to wash, to clean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Wasn't such a thing. Within-- within-- in-- in a few weeks, everybody was full of lice. Full of lice. Although they sprayed us with-- with a compound like petroleum. Maybe it was petroleum. After we took our shower, we were sprayed with a-- like a flit gun. And it smelled like petroleum. That smell didn't go away for weeks. And it was burning also.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they shave your heads at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: We already had shaved heads, but in the last four weeks, it grew. And they didn't want that. They-- like-- like-- we were shaved with-- with a-- with a razor blade. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Their language, the thoughts, what were they talking about.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you were here, what kinds of things you were talking to the other people?

speaker: interviewee
text: First, it's amazing. If people are starved, you talk all day long of food. It's almost like a disease, like a sickness. And-- and-- and some even tried to-- to imagine that they were eating good food. They were talking very illustrated about I went to this restaurant. My mother was a good cook. To satisfy our-- our-- our burning hunger with-- with images. With-- with illusions.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then again, we talked about it over and over again. Will they let us live? That was the-- the number one cardinal question, because there was no guarantee that one day they will decide we don't need people. We don't need to feed them. And-- and we knew a flick decision just like this.

speaker: interviewee
text: The [GERMAN] at that time, it was Rudolf Hoess-- not Hess, Hoess-- that he might decide there are too many people. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had a feeling here that the decision to put people in the gas chambers were literally dependent how many new transports arrive. And there was only a limited capacity. They could only house so many people. So we-- we were literally praying, I hope they don't bring any newcomers. That I remember also, we were telling. I hope they don't bring in newcomers. There is room for us to stay alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when we were transferred to Buna, to Auschwitz III, at least we had that feeling that there's a good chance if we work, we will survive a little longer. The question was, how long can you withstand the chicanery, the hard work, and the lack of food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because when you don't have enough food and you get emaciated, people get sick. They're full of boils, abscess. And if that happened, God forbid, that was already literally a step further to the gas chambers.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Let's finish. He said that there was no--

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, yeah. You want to-- is he going to pan out in shot? [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Michelle. [NON-ENGLISH]. Michelle, he said that there--
